questions.question:
Which of the following suffices to convert an arbitrary CFG to an LL(1) grammar?

A. Removing left recursion alone
B. Factoring the grammar alone
C. Removing left recursion and factoring the grammar
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Which transformation(s) are sufficient to convert any CFG into an LL(1) grammar.

understand.plain: LL(1) parsers require the grammar to have no left recursion and no common prefixes among alternatives (left-factored). However, even after removing left recursion and performing left-factoring, some CFGs still cannot become LL(1) because they are inherently ambiguous or have deep structural issues. So no finite set of transformations guarantees conversion of an arbitrary CFG to LL(1).

keywords.explain: A grammar rule of the form A -> A alpha, where the non-terminal derives itself at the leftmost position. This causes infinite loops in top-down parsers.
keywords.example: E -> E + T (left-recursive) must be rewritten to E -> T E', E' -> + T E' | epsilon

keywords.explain: Transformation to eliminate common prefixes in productions for the same non-terminal, so the parser can decide which production to use with one lookahead token.
keywords.example: A -> alpha beta | alpha gamma becomes A -> alpha A', A' -> beta | gamma

keywords.explain: A grammar that can be parsed top-down with one token of lookahead, with no backtracking. The FIRST and FOLLOW sets must not conflict.
keywords.example: A grammar with no left recursion and no common prefixes may still not be LL(1) if the FIRST/FOLLOW sets of alternatives overlap.

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram concept: LL(1) grammars are a strict subset of CFGs; removing left recursion and left-factoring can move a grammar closer to LL(1) but cannot guarantee membership.

given.aim: Determine which transformation(s) guarantee converting any CFG to LL(1).

terms.meaning: Any context-free grammar, including ambiguous ones or those with constructs that fundamentally prevent LL(1) parsing.
terms.example: The grammar for arithmetic expressions with left recursion is an arbitrary CFG.
terms.connects: We need to determine if left recursion removal and/or left-factoring can always make such a grammar LL(1).

terms.meaning: Necessary condition for LL(1): eliminates cases where the parser loops forever, but does not guarantee LL(1) alone.
terms.example: E -> E+T | T becomes E -> T E', E' -> +T E' | epsilon
terms.connects: Required but not sufficient; FIRST/FOLLOW conflicts may still exist.

terms.meaning: Necessary condition for LL(1): ensures no two alternatives for the same non-terminal share a common prefix token.
terms.example: S -> if E then S else S | if E then S
terms.connects: Required but not sufficient; dangling-else style ambiguity cannot be fully resolved this way.

given.plan: Recall that LL(1) grammars form a strict subset of unambiguous CFGs. Even after removing left recursion and left-factoring, an inherently ambiguous grammar (or one that is unambiguous but not LL(1)) will still fail the LL(1) parse table test (conflicting entries). Therefore, neither transformation alone nor both together are sufficient in the general case.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: Removing left recursion alone
steps.apply: Left recursion removal is necessary for top-down parsing (to avoid infinite loops), but it does not address common prefixes. A grammar can be free of left recursion yet still have two productions for the same non-terminal that start with the same terminal, causing FIRST-set conflicts. So A is insufficient.
steps.note: Example: A -> alpha beta | alpha gamma has no left recursion but needs left-factoring.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: Left factoring alone
steps.apply: Left factoring removes common prefixes so the parser can deterministically choose a production with one lookahead. However, if left recursion still exists, the top-down parser loops. Moreover, left factoring a left-recursive grammar before removing recursion does not make it LL(1). So B is insufficient.
steps.note: Left factoring and left recursion removal must both be done, but even that is not always enough.

steps.title: Evaluate option C: Both transformations together
steps.apply: Applying both removes left recursion and common prefixes — these are necessary conditions for LL(1). However, they are not sufficient. Consider the grammar for 'dangling-else' or any inherently ambiguous grammar. After both transformations, conflicts in the LL(1) parse table (FIRST/FOLLOW overlaps caused by structural ambiguity) may remain. A counterexample: S -> if E then S else S | if E then S cannot be made LL(1) by these transforms alone. So C is also not universally sufficient.
steps.note: The class of LL(1) grammars is a proper subset of all CFGs; no algorithm can always convert an arbitrary CFG to LL(1).

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: None of options A, B, or C is universally sufficient to convert an arbitrary CFG to LL(1). The answer is D.
steps.note: For specific grammars (not arbitrary), both transformations together often do produce an LL(1) grammar, but not in the general case.

solution.result: D. None of the above — no combination of left recursion removal and left-factoring is guaranteed to convert an arbitrary CFG to LL(1).

questions.formula_note: LL(1) grammar conditions: no left recursion, no common prefixes, and disjoint FIRST/FOLLOW sets for each non-terminal's alternatives. These are necessary but not always achievable from an arbitrary CFG.

questions.question:
Consider the following expression grammar. The semantic rules for expression evaluation are stated next to each grammar production:

  E = number     | E.val = number.val
  | E '+' E     | E^(1).val = E^(2).val + E^(3).val
  | E 'x' E     | E^(1).val = E^(2).val x E^(3).val

The above grammar and the semantic rules are fed to a yacc tool (which is an LALR(1) parser generator) for parsing and evaluating arithmetic expressions. Which one of the following describes the conflicts it detects?

A. It detects reduce-reduce conflict, and eliminates the conflicts.
B. It detects shift-reduce conflicts, and resolves the conflict in favor of a shift over a reduce action.
C. It detects shift-reduce conflicts, and resolves the conflict in favor of a reduce over a shift action.
D. It detects reduce-reduce conflict, and resolves the conflict in favor of a reduce over a shift action.
questions.answer: B. It detects shift-reduce conflicts, and resolves the conflict in favor of a shift over a reduce action.
questions.to_find: The type of parsing conflict yacc detects in the given ambiguous expression grammar and how it resolves them by default.

understand.plain: The grammar E -> E '+' E | E 'x' E | number is ambiguous because it does not specify precedence or associativity for '+' and 'x'. When yacc (an LALR(1) parser generator) encounters such a grammar, it finds shift-reduce conflicts in the parsing tables. By default, yacc resolves these in favor of shift over reduce, which effectively makes the operators right-associative and gives equal precedence to all operators.

keywords.explain: Occurs in an LALR(1) parse table when, for a given state and lookahead token, both a shift action and a reduce action are valid. This happens with ambiguous grammars for operators.
keywords.example: After parsing E + E with '+' as lookahead: should we reduce E+E to E (reduce), or shift the new '+' (shift)?

keywords.explain: When yacc encounters a shift-reduce conflict without explicit precedence declarations, it resolves by preferring shift over reduce.
keywords.example: For E + E with '+' ahead: yacc shifts, making '+' right-associative.

keywords.explain: A grammar for which a string can have more than one parse tree. The grammar E -> E op E is ambiguous because it does not encode precedence or associativity.
keywords.example: 3 + 2 x 1 can parse as (3+2)x1 or 3+(2x1) in an ambiguous grammar.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='460' height='80' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Shift-Reduce Conflict Example</text><text x='240' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Stack: ... E + E   Lookahead: +</text><text x='120' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Reduce: E+E -> E</text><text x='360' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Shift: push + onto stack</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='215' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='255' y='110' width='215' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='118' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Left-assoc (not chosen)</text><text x='362' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Yacc default: SHIFT wins</text><text x='240' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Result: right-associative, equal precedence</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A diagram showing that when the stack has E+E and the lookahead is +, yacc must choose between reducing (left-assoc) or shifting (right-assoc). By default yacc shifts.

given.aim: Identify the conflict type in the yacc-processed ambiguous expression grammar and yacc's default resolution strategy.

terms.term: Grammar E -> E '+' E | E 'x' E | number
terms.meaning: An ambiguous grammar that does not encode operator precedence or associativity.
terms.example: For input 3 + 2 x 1, the grammar allows two parse trees.
terms.connects: Ambiguity leads to multiple valid parser actions for the same state and lookahead.

terms.meaning: A bottom-up parser that uses one lookahead token. It processes the grammar into action/goto tables; conflicts appear as multiple entries in the same cell.
terms.example: Shift and reduce both valid for state S5 with lookahead '+' gives a shift-reduce conflict.
terms.connects: The conflict type (shift-reduce vs reduce-reduce) determines the answer.

terms.meaning: Yacc (Yet Another Compiler Compiler) resolves shift-reduce conflicts by choosing shift, and reduce-reduce conflicts by choosing the rule listed first in the grammar.
terms.example: Without %left or %right declarations, '+' is treated as right-associative by yacc's default shift preference.
terms.connects: The default shift preference is the key to answering this question correctly.

given.plan: Determine what kind of conflict an ambiguous grammar of the form E -> E op E produces in an LALR(1) parser. Then recall yacc's default conflict resolution policy.

steps.title: Identify the conflict type for E -> E op E
steps.apply: Consider parsing the string 'a + b + c'. After shifting 'a', '+', 'b', the parser has stack: E + E and lookahead '+'. Two actions are possible: (1) Reduce E+E to E (then shift '+'), or (2) Shift '+' (then continue). Both are valid under the ambiguous grammar. This is a shift-reduce conflict — not a reduce-reduce conflict (no two different rules both apply for a reduce).
steps.note: Reduce-reduce conflicts arise when two different grammar rules can both reduce the same right-hand side. That is not the case here.

steps.title: Recall yacc's default shift-reduce resolution
steps.apply: By default (without explicit %left, %right, %nonassoc, %prec declarations), yacc resolves shift-reduce conflicts in favor of shift. This makes all operators right-associative with equal precedence. So yacc detects shift-reduce conflicts and resolves them by choosing shift over reduce.
steps.note: Option B exactly describes this behavior. Option C (resolve in favor of reduce) is incorrect — that would be the opposite of yacc's default.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A says reduce-reduce conflict and eliminates — wrong conflict type. C says shift-reduce but resolves in favor of reduce — opposite of yacc default. D says reduce-reduce and resolve reduce over shift — both parts wrong. Only B is correct: shift-reduce conflict resolved by preferring shift.
steps.note: Understanding the distinction between shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts is critical for GATE.

solution.result: B. It detects shift-reduce conflicts, and resolves the conflict in favor of a shift over a reduce action.

questions.formula_note: Yacc default conflict resolution: shift-reduce conflict -> prefer shift (right-associativity); reduce-reduce conflict -> prefer the rule that appears first in the grammar file.

questions.question:
Consider the following expression grammar. The semantic rules for expression evaluation are stated next to each grammar production:

  E = number     | E.val = number.val
  | E '+' E     | E^(1).val = E^(2).val + E^(3).val
  | E 'x' E     | E^(1).val = E^(2).val x E^(3).val

Assume the conflicts in this grammar are resolved using yacc tool and an LALR(1) parser is generated for parsing arithmetic expressions as per the given grammar. Consider an expression 3 x 2 - 1. What precedence and associativity does the generated parser realize?

A. Equal precedence and left associativity; expression is evaluated to 7
B. Equal precedence and right associativity; expression is evaluated to 9
C. Precedence of 'x' is higher than that of '+'; and both operators are left associative; expression is evaluated to 5
D. Precedence of 'x' is higher than that of '+', and both operators are left associative; expression is evaluated to 3
questions.answer: B. Equal precedence and right associativity; expression is evaluated to 9
questions.to_find: What precedence and associativity the yacc-generated parser assigns to '+' and 'x', and the value of the expression 3 x 2 - 1 (where '-' represents subtraction but in the image context the expression is 3 x 2 + 1, let us read carefully) under those rules.

understand.plain: Since yacc resolves shift-reduce conflicts by preferring shift (as established in the previous part), all operators get equal precedence and are treated as right-associative. For the expression 3 x 2 - 1 (interpreted as 3 x (2 - 1) is wrong since '-' is not in the grammar; the image shows 3 x 2 - 1 where the minus sign visually is a subtraction but the grammar only has '+' and 'x'; however the expression in the image is '3 x 2 - 1' and since '-' is not in the grammar, this is likely '3 x 2 + 1' or the '-' is a typographic artifact for subtraction). Reading carefully: the image shows the expression as 3 x 2 - 1. With right-associativity and equal precedence, the parser evaluates 3 x (2 - 1) ... but '-' is not in the grammar. The question uses '-' as subtraction contextually; however based on the options, evaluation to 9 suggests 3 x (2 + 1) = 9, so the expression is likely 3 x 2 + 1 parsed as 3 x (2+1) = 9 under right-associativity.

keywords.explain: When yacc shifts on a shift-reduce conflict, the rightmost sub-expression is grouped first. So a op b op c is parsed as a op (b op c).
keywords.example: 3 x 2 + 1 with right assoc and equal precedence: 3 x (2 + 1) = 3 x 3 = 9

keywords.explain: Both '+' and 'x' are treated with the same precedence level since no %prec or ordering declarations were given to yacc.
keywords.example: 3 x 2 + 1: since x and + have equal precedence and are right-associative, we evaluate the rightmost operation first: 2 + 1 = 3, then 3 x 3 = 9.

keywords.explain: Yacc prefers shift over reduce in shift-reduce conflicts. This means when deciding between reducing the current handle or shifting the next operator, yacc shifts — delaying reduction and effectively grouping to the right.
keywords.example: Stack E x E, lookahead '+': yacc shifts '+' rather than reducing E x E, leading to E x (E + E) grouping.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='40' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Parse tree: 3 x 2 + 1 (right-associative, equal precedence)</text><line x1='250' y1='75' x2='150' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='75' x2='250' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='75' x2='350' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='250' cy='70' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>x</text><circle cx='150' cy='120' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>3</text><circle cx='250' cy='120' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>+</text><circle cx='350' cy='120' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>...</text><line x1='250' y1='138' x2='210' y2='168' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='138' x2='290' y2='168' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='210' cy='175' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='181' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>2</text><circle cx='290' cy='175' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='181' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>1</text><text x='250' y='212' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 x (2 + 1) = 3 x 3 = 9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree for 3 x (2 + 1) showing right-associative grouping. The root is 'x' with children 3 and the subtree '2+1'. Result = 3 x 3 = 9.

given.aim: Determine what value the yacc-generated parser computes for the expression 3 x 2 + 1, given that it uses default shift-preference (right-associativity, equal precedence).

terms.meaning: An arithmetic expression using the two operators in the grammar. With equal precedence and right associativity, it is parsed as 3 x (2 + 1).
terms.example: Left-assoc equal prec: (3 x 2) + 1 = 7. Right-assoc equal prec: 3 x (2+1) = 9.
terms.connects: The choice of associativity directly determines the numeric result.

terms.meaning: No %left or %right declarations means yacc treats '+' and 'x' at the same precedence level — neither binds tighter.
terms.example: In normal math, x has higher precedence than + so 3 x 2 + 1 = 7. With equal precedence this changes.
terms.connects: Equal precedence means the associativity rule (right by default) determines the grouping.

terms.meaning: When the parser has E x E on stack and sees '+', it shifts (right-assoc). The '+' subtree becomes the right child of 'x', so the right side is evaluated first.
terms.connects: This gives the final computed value of 9, matching option B.

given.plan: Apply the result from the previous part: yacc gives equal precedence and right-associativity to all operators. Parse 3 x 2 + 1 right-to-left in terms of grouping: 3 x (2 + 1) = 3 x 3 = 9.

steps.title: Establish precedence and associativity from yacc defaults
steps.apply: From the previous part (Q3.17.13), yacc resolves shift-reduce conflicts by preferring shift. This means: (1) Both '+' and 'x' have equal precedence (no ordering declared), and (2) Both are right-associative (shift preference delays reduction, grouping right).
steps.note: Normal mathematical convention gives 'x' higher precedence than '+', but the yacc default ignores this.

steps.title: Parse the expression 3 x 2 + 1 with equal-precedence right-associativity
steps.apply:
With equal precedence and right-associativity:
- Stack: E x E, lookahead: '+'
- Yacc shifts '+' (shift-reduce conflict resolved by shift)
- Stack: E x E + E (after shifting and reducing 1 to E)
- Now reduce E+E to E: value = 2+1 = 3
- Stack: E x E (where second E has value 3)
- Reduce E x E to E: value = 3 x 3 = 9
So 3 x 2 + 1 is parsed as 3 x (2 + 1) = 9.
steps.note: If left-associative with equal precedence, the result would be (3 x 2) + 1 = 7, which is option A.

steps.title: Match to the given options
steps.apply: Option B states: 'Equal precedence and right associativity; expression is evaluated to 9.' This matches exactly: equal precedence (no declarations), right-associative (shift preference), result = 9.
steps.note: Options C and D describe normal mathematical precedence (x higher than +), which would require explicit %left/%right declarations in yacc.

solution.result: B. Equal precedence and right associativity; expression is evaluated to 9.

questions.formula_note: Yacc default: shift-reduce conflict resolved by shift -> right-associativity for all undeclared operators with equal precedence. For 3 x (2+1): result = 3 x 3 = 9.
